Problem Statement¶

Business Context¶

The advent of e-news, or electronic news, portals has offered us a great opportunity to quickly get updates on the day-to-day events occurring globally. The information on these portals is retrieved electronically from online databases, processed using a variety of software, and then transmitted to the users. There are multiple advantages of transmitting news electronically, like faster access to the content and the ability to utilize different technologies such as audio, graphics, video, and other interactive elements that are either not being used or aren’t common yet in traditional newspapers. E-news Express, an online news portal, aims to expand its business by acquiring new subscribers. With every visitor to the website taking certain actions based on their interest, the company plans to analyze these actions to understand user interests and determine how to drive better engagement. The executives at E-news Express believe that there has been a decline in new monthly subscribers compared to the past year because the current webpage is not designed well enough in terms of the outline & recommended content to keep customers engaged long enough to decide to subscribe.

[Companies often analyze user responses to two variants of a product to decide which of the two variants is more effective. This experimental technique, known as A/B testing, is used to determine whether a new feature attracts users based on a chosen metric.]

Objective¶

The design team of the company has researched and created a new landing page that has a new outline & more relevant content compared to the old page. To test the effectiveness of the new landing page in gathering new subscribers, the Data Science team experimented by randomly selecting 100 users and dividing them equally into two groups. The existing landing page was served to the first group (control group) and the new landing page to the second group (treatment group). Data regarding the interaction of users in both groups with the two versions of the landing page was collected.

Being a data scientist in E-news Express, you have been asked to explore the data and perform a statistical analysis (at a significance level of 5%) to determine the effectiveness of the new landing page in gathering new subscribers for the news portal by answering the following questions:

  1. Do the users spend more time on the new landing page than on the existing landing page?
  2. Is the conversion rate (the proportion of users who visit the landing page and get converted) for the new page greater than the conversion rate for the old page?
  3. Does the converted status depend on the preferred language?
  4. Is the time spent on the new page the same for the different language users?

Data Dictionary¶

The data contains information regarding the interaction of users in both groups with the two versions of the landing page. user_id - Unique user ID of the person visiting the website

  • group - Whether the user belongs to the first group (control) or the second group (treatment)
  • landing_page - Whether the landing page is new or old
  • time_spent_on_the_page - Time (in minutes) spent by the user on the landing page
  • converted - Whether the user gets converted to a subscriber of the news portal or not
  • language_preferred - language chosen by the user to view the landing page

Solution Approach¶

The solution approach will involve the following steps:

  1. Data Loading and Initial Inspection: Load the dataset and perform an initial inspection to understand its structure, check for missing values, duplicates, and data types.
  2. Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA): Conduct both univariate and bivariate analyses to understand the distribution of variables, identify patterns, and visualize relationships between the group, landing_page, time_spent_on_the_page, converted, and language_preferred columns.
  3. Hypothesis Testing for Question 1: Determine if users spend more time on the new landing page. This will involve:
    • Formulating null and alternative hypotheses.
    • Selection of the appropriate Hypothesis Test.
    • Checking assumptions for the chosen test.
    • Calculating the test statistic and p-value.
    • Drawing conclusions based on the p-value and a significance level of 0.05.
  4. Hypothesis Testing for Question 2: Determine if the conversion rate for the new page is greater than for the old page. This will involve:
    • Formulating null and alternative hypotheses.
    • Selection of the appropriate Hypothesis Test.
    • Checking assumptions for the chosen test.
    • Calculating the test statistic and p-value.
    • Drawing conclusions.
  5. Hypothesis Testing for Question 3: Investigate if the converted status depend on the preferred language.
    • Formulating null and alternative hypotheses.
    • Selection of the appropriate Hypothesis Test.
    • Checking assumptions for the chosen test.
    • Calculating the test statistic and p-value.
    • Drawing conclusions.
  6. Hypothesis Testing for Question 4: Check if the mean time spent on the new page is the same for different language users (within the treatment group).
    • Formulating null and alternative hypotheses.
    • Selection of the appropriate Hypothesis Test.
    • Checking assumptions for the chosen test.
    • Calculating the test statistic and p-value.
    • Drawing conclusions.
  7. Actionable Insights and Conclusions: Summarize all findings and provide practical recommendations based on the statistical analysis.

Analytical Approach for Selection of Hypothesis Test:¶

  • Independent Samples t-test: The selection of this statistical test would be based on the type of variables (continuous dependent, categorical independent), the number of groups, and the hypothesis (comparison of means).
  • Two-proportion One-Tailed Z-test: The selection of the appropriate statistical test will be based on the type of variables (categorical dependent, categorical independent), the number of groups, the hypothesis (comparison of proportions), and the alternative Hypothesis (one-tailed or two-tailed).
  • Chi-square test for independence: The selection of the appropriate statistical test will be based on the type of variables (two categorical variables) and the hypothesis (test of independence).
  • One-way ANOVA: The selection of the appropriate statistical test will be based on the type of variables (continuous dependent, categorical independent with more than two groups) and the hypothesis (comparison of means across multiple groups).

Data Overview¶

The initial steps to get an overview of any dataset is to:

  • observe the first few rows of the dataset, to check whether the dataset has been loaded properly or not
  • get information about the number of rows and columns in the dataset
  • find out the data types of the columns to ensure that data is stored in the preferred format and the value of each property is as expected.
  • check the statistical summary of the dataset to get an overview of the numerical columns of the data

Observations¶

  • The user_id is a column consisting of unique user-ids. This is of no consequence for the current study and, hence, has been dropped later.
  • The mean engagement-time is 5.3778 minutes, while the median engagement-time is 5.415 minutes.
  • There are 2 groups (control and treatment), with equal no of samples in each, who are getting the old and new landing pages respectively.
  • There are 3 unique values for language and 2 unique values for converted columns.

Exploratory Data Analysis¶

Out[114]:
<Axes: xlabel='time_spent_on_the_page', ylabel='Count'>

Observations¶

Observations from Univariate Analysis¶

  • The time_spent_on_the_page column appears to be somewhat symmetrically distributed, with a mean of approximately 5.38 minutes and a median of 5.42 minutes. The range is from 0.19 to 10.71 minutes.
  • There are an equal number of users (50 each) in the control and treatment groups.
  • Similarly, there are an equal number of old and new landing_page views (50 each).
  • More users were converted (54) than not converted (46).
  • The language_preferred distribution is fairly balanced, with Spanish (34) and French (34) having slightly more users than English (32).

Bivariate Analysis¶

In [115]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10,15))
plt.suptitle('Bivariate Analysis',fontsize=20)
for i, feature in enumerate(cat_cols):
    plt.subplot(4,1,i+1)
    sns.boxplot(data,x='group',y='time_spent_on_the_page',hue=feature)
    plt.title(feature)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [116]:
#Plotting groups vs conversion
plt.figure(figsize=(10,10))
plt.suptitle('Bivariate Analysis',fontsize=20)
for i, feature in enumerate(cat_cols):
    plt.subplot(4,1,i+1)
    sns.countplot(data,x='group',hue=feature)
    plt.title(feature)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Observations¶

Observations from Bivariate Analysis¶

  • Group and Landing Page vs. Time Spent: Users in the treatment group (exposed to the new landing page) generally spent more time on the page compared to users in the control group (exposed to the old landing page). The median time spent for the treatment group appears higher, suggesting the new page might be more engaging.
  • Converted Status vs. Time Spent: Across both control and treatment groups, users who converted (yes) tend to have spent considerably more time on the page than those who not converted (no). This indicates a positive correlation between time spent and conversion.
  • Language Preferred vs. Time Spent (within groups): The time spent on the page appears to be relatively consistent across different language_preferred categories within each group (control/treatment). There might be minor variations, but no drastically different patterns are immediately obvious, suggesting language preference might not be a strong predictor of time spent, at least in a simple bivariate context.
  • Group vs. Landing Page: As expected from the experimental setup, all users in the control group were shown the old landing page, and all users in the treatment group were shown the new landing page.
  • Group vs. Converted Status: In the control group, there were more users who not converted compared to those who converted. Conversely, in the treatment group, there were more users who converted compared to those who not converted. This visually suggests that the new landing page (treatment group) might lead to a higher conversion rate.
  • Group vs. Language Preferred: The distribution of language_preferred (English, Spanish, French) appears to be well-balanced and similar between the control and treatment groups, indicating a successful randomization of participants based on language preference.

Question 1: Do the users spend more time on the new landing page than the old landing page?¶

To visualize the distribution of time-spent-on-page, we can plot a distribution plot.

In [117]:
# plot the distribution plot of time-spent-on-the-page
plt.figure(figsize=(5,5))
sns.displot(data=data, x='time_spent_on_the_page', kde=True, hue='group')
plt.show()

<Figure size 500x500 with 0 Axes>
In [118]:
# plot the boxplot of time-spent-on-the-page by the users of control and treatment groups
plt.figure(figsize=(5,5))
sns.boxplot(data=data, x='group', y='time_spent_on_the_page')
plt.show()

Another relevant visualization: Bar Plot of Mean Time Spent with Confidence Intervals¶

In [119]:
plt.figure(figsize=(7, 5))
sns.barplot(x='group', y='time_spent_on_the_page', data=data, errorbar=('ci',95))
plt.title('Mean Time Spent on Page by Group with 95% Confidence Intervals')
plt.xlabel('Group')
plt.ylabel('Mean Time Spent on Page (minutes)')
plt.show()

Observations from Distribution Plot¶

  • The distribution of time_spent_on_the_page for both the control and treatment groups appears somewhat symmetric, though there might be a slight right skew for both.
  • Visually, the treatment group's distribution seems to be shifted slightly to the right compared to the control group, indicating that users in the new landing page group (treatment) might be spending more time on the page on average than those in the old landing page group (control).
  • Both groups exhibit a similar spread in the time spent, suggesting no major differences in variability between them based on this plot.

Observations from Box Plot¶

  • The median time_spent_on_the_page for the treatment group appears noticeably higher than that for the control group, reinforcing the idea that users on the new landing page spend more time.
  • Both groups show a similar interquartile range (IQR), indicating comparable variability in the central 50% of the data. However, the control group seems to have a slightly wider spread, especially towards the lower end.
  • There are no obvious outliers in either group, suggesting that the data is relatively clean and consistent within each group.

Observations from Bar Plot of Mean Time Spent¶

  • The bar plot clearly shows that the mean time_spent_on_the_page for the treatment group is higher than for the control group.
  • The confidence intervals (error bars) for the two groups do not overlap, which visually suggests a statistically significant difference between the means. This supports the hypothesis that the new landing page leads to increased user engagement in terms of time spent.

Step 1: Define null and alternative hypotheses¶

Let's frame the null and alternative hypothesis

$H_0:$ The mean time_spent_on_the_page of Control group is equal to that of Treatment group.

$H_a:$ The mean time_spent_on_the_page of Control group is less than that of Treatment group.

Let $\mu_1$ be the mean app engagement time of Control group Let $\mu_2$ be the mean app engagement time of Treatment group

Mathematically, the above formulated hypotheses can be written as:

$H_0: \mu_1 \ge \mu_2 $
$H_a: \mu_1 < \mu_2 $

In [120]:
# find the sample means and sample standard deviations for the two groups
control_mean = data[data['group'] == 'control']['time_spent_on_the_page'].mean()
treatment_mean = data[data['group'] == 'treatment']['time_spent_on_the_page'].mean()
print("Control Group Mean:",control_mean)
print("Treatment Group Mean:",treatment_mean)
print("-----------------------")
control_sd = data[data['group'] == 'control']['time_spent_on_the_page'].std()
treatment_sd = data[data['group'] == 'treatment']['time_spent_on_the_page'].std()
print("Control Group Standard Deviation:",control_sd)
print("Treatment Group Standard Deviation:",treatment_sd)

Control Group Mean: 4.532400000000001
Treatment Group Mean: 6.2232
-----------------------
Control Group Standard Deviation: 2.581974849306046
Treatment Group Standard Deviation: 1.8170310387878263

Step 2: Select Appropriate test¶

This is a case of a one-tailed test for the comparison of the mean for 2 independent groups. As the standard deviations of the two groups are different, selecting the 2-sample independent t-test for the Hypothesis test as the appropriate one.

Let's test whether the T-test assumptions are satisfied or not

  • Continuous data - Yes, the usage time is measured on a continuous scale.
  • Normally distributed populations - Yes, the groups each have sample size of 50 (n>30) and, hence, can be taken to be normal distributions
  • Independent populations - As we are taking random samples for two different type of users, the two samples are from two independent populations.
  • Unequal population standard deviations - As the sample standard deviations are different, the population standard deviations may be assumed to be different.
  • Random sampling from the population - Yes, we are informed that the collected samples are random samples.

We can use two sample T-test for this problem.

Prepare the Data for the 2-Sample Independent t-test¶

In [121]:
#Fetch the time_spent_on_the_page by the control group into a new series
control_group_time = data[data['group'] == 'control']['time_spent_on_the_page']
treatment_group_time = data[data['group'] == 'treatment']['time_spent_on_the_page']
print(control_group_time.head(5))
print(treatment_group_time.head(5))

0    3.48
3    3.02
5    5.28
7    6.53
9    2.08
Name: time_spent_on_the_page, dtype: float64
1     7.13
2     4.40
4     4.75
6     5.25
8    10.71
Name: time_spent_on_the_page, dtype: float64

Step 3: Decide the significance level¶

As mentioned in the Business Context the level of significane (alpha) has been decided as 0.05

Step 4: Calculate the p-value¶

The ttest_ind() function of Scipy will be used to compute the test statistic and p-value.

In [122]:
#import the required functions
from scipy.stats import ttest_ind

# find the p-value
test_stat, p_value = ttest_ind(control_group_time, treatment_group_time, equal_var = False, alternative = 'less')
print('The p-value is ', p_value)

The p-value is  0.0001392381225166549

Step 5: Compare the p-value with $\alpha$ (level of significance)¶

In [123]:
# print the conclusion based on p-value
if p_value < 0.05:
    print(f'As the p-value {p_value} is less than the level of significance, we reject the null hypothesis.')
else:
    print(f'As the p-value {p_value} is greater than the level of significance, we fail to reject the null hypothesis.')

As the p-value 0.0001392381225166549 is less than the level of significance, we reject the null hypothesis.

Step 6: Draw Inference¶

Since the p-value is less than the 5% significance level, we reject the null hypothesis. Hence, we have enough statistical evidence to say that the mean time_spent_on_the_page of the control group is less than that of the treatment group.

In other words, users are, on an average, spending more time on the new landing page than the old landing page.

Question 2: Is the conversion rate (the proportion of users who visit the landing page and get converted) for the new page greater than the conversion rate for the old page?¶

In [124]:
#fetching the count of converted='yes' users by group
data.groupby(['group','converted'])['converted'].count()

/tmp/ipykernel_913/2819203932.py:2: FutureWarning: The default of observed=False is deprecated and will be changed to True in a future version of pandas. Pass observed=False to retain current behavior or observed=True to adopt the future default and silence this warning.
  data.groupby(['group','converted'])['converted'].count()
Out[124]:
converted
group converted
control no 29
yes 21
treatment no 17
yes 33

Since both the concerned variables (group,converted) are categorical in nature, we can use a contingency table and a stacked bar graph to visually inspect the data.

The pandas crosstab() function is used to create a contingency table between two categorical variables from a dataframe. The syntax of the function is:

pd.crosstab(index, columns, margins=False, normalize=False)

where

  • index: Values to group by in the rows
  • columns: Values to group by in the columns
  • margins: Whether to add row/column sub-totals. Default value is False
  • normalize: Whether to normalize the value-counts. Default value is False
In [125]:
# create a contingency table to tabulate the frequencies of group and converted
contingency_table = pd.crosstab(data.group, data.converted)
contingency_table
Out[125]:
converted no yes
group
control 29 21
treatment 17 33

To visualize the relationship between the two categorical variables, we can plot a stacked bar chart.

In [126]:
# visual analysis
pd.crosstab(data.group, data.converted,normalize=True).plot(kind='bar',stacked=True)
Out[126]:
<Axes: xlabel='group'>

Observations¶

Observations from Contingency Table and Stacked Bar Chart¶

  • Control Group Conversion: In the control group (old landing page), 21 out of 50 users converted, which is a conversion rate of 42%.
  • Treatment Group Conversion: In the treatment group (new landing page), 33 out of 50 users converted, resulting in a conversion rate of 66%.
  • Comparison: The treatment group exhibits a substantially higher conversion rate (66%) compared to the control group (42%). This visual analysis suggests that the new landing page is more effective in converting users into subscribers.

Step 1: Define null and alternative hypotheses¶

Let's formulate the null and alternative hypothesis for the above problem

$H_0:$ The conversion rate of the control group is greater than or equal compared to treatment group

$H_a:$ The conversion rate of the control group is lesser than the treatment group

Let $p1,p2$ be the proportions of converted users from the control and treatment groups respectively.

$H_0:p_1 \ge p_2$

against the alternate hypothesis

$H_a:p_1 < p_2$

Step 2: Select Appropriate test¶

To test whether the conversion frequency is greater for the treatment group compared to the control group, a Two-proportion one-tailed z-test is being selected.

Let's test whether the Z-test assumptions are satisfied or not¶

  • Binomally distributed population - Yes, a user is either converted or not.
  • Random sampling from the population - Yes, we are informed that the collected sample is a simple random sample.
  • Can the binomial distribution approximated to normal distribution - Yes. For binary data, CLT works slower than usual. The standard thing is to check whether np and n(1-p) are greater than or equal to 10. Here, n and p refer to the sample size and sample proportion respectively.

    $np_1 = 50\cdot \frac{21}{50} =21 \geq 10\\ n(1-p_1) = 50 \cdot \frac{50-21}{50} =29 \geq 10 \\ np_2 = 50\cdot \frac{33}{50} =33 \geq 10\\ n(1-p_2) = 50 \cdot \frac{50-33}{50} =17 \geq 10 $

Step 3: Decide the significance level¶

As mentioned in the Business Context the level of significane (alpha) has been decided as 0.05

Step 4: Collect and prepare data¶

Let's prepare the data for the test.

In [127]:
# prepare the data for the 2-proportion z-test
contingency_table
Out[127]:
converted no yes
group
control 29 21
treatment 17 33

Step 5: Calculate the p-value¶

This test uses the proportions_ztest() function. The function will be used for comparing two proportions.

In [128]:
# import the required fuction
from statsmodels.stats.proportion import proportions_ztest

# set the counts of converted users for control and treatment groups
converted_count = np.array([21, 33])

# set the sample sizes
nobs = np.array([50, 50])

# find the p-value
test_stat, p_value = proportions_ztest(converted_count, nobs, alternative='smaller')
print('The p-value is ' + str(p_value))

The p-value is 0.008026308204056278

Step 6: Compare the p-value with $\alpha$¶

In [129]:
# print the conclusion based on p-value
if p_value < 0.05:
    print(f'As the p-value {p_value} is less than the level of significance, we reject the null hypothesis.')
else:
    print(f'As the p-value {p_value} is greater than the level of significance, we fail to reject the null hypothesis.')

As the p-value 0.008026308204056278 is less than the level of significance, we reject the null hypothesis.

Step 7: Draw inference¶

Since the p-value is less than the 5% significance level, we reject the null hypothesis.

Hence, we have enough statistical evidence to say that the treatment group has a higher conversion proportion compared to the control group.

Question 3: Does the converted status depend on the preferred language?¶

In [130]:
#fetching the count of converted='yes' users by language_preferred
data.groupby(['language_preferred','converted'])['converted'].count()

/tmp/ipykernel_913/200686053.py:2: FutureWarning: The default of observed=False is deprecated and will be changed to True in a future version of pandas. Pass observed=False to retain current behavior or observed=True to adopt the future default and silence this warning.
  data.groupby(['language_preferred','converted'])['converted'].count()
Out[130]:
converted
language_preferred converted
English no 11
yes 21
French no 19
yes 15
Spanish no 16
yes 18

Since both the concerned variables (language_preferred,converted) are categorical in nature, we can use a contingency table and a stacked bar graph to visually inspect the data.

In [131]:
# create a contingency table to tabulate the frequencies of group and converted
contingency_table_2 = pd.crosstab(data.language_preferred, data.converted)
contingency_table_2
Out[131]:
converted no yes
language_preferred
English 11 21
French 19 15
Spanish 16 18

To visualize the relationship between the two categorical variables, we can plot a stacked bar chart.

In [132]:
# visual analysis
pd.crosstab(data.language_preferred, data.converted,normalize=True).plot(kind='bar',stacked=True)
Out[132]:
<Axes: xlabel='language_preferred'>

Observations from Contingency Table and Stacked Bar Chart for Language Preferred vs. Converted Status¶

  • English Conversion: Out of 32 users who preferred English, 21 converted, resulting in a conversion rate of approximately 65.6%.
  • French Conversion: Out of 34 users who preferred French, 15 converted, leading to a conversion rate of approximately 44.1%.
  • Spanish Conversion: Out of 34 users who preferred Spanish, 18 converted, for a conversion rate of approximately 52.9%.
  • Comparison: English-speaking users show the highest conversion rate, followed by Spanish, and then French. This suggests a potential dependency between the preferred language and the likelihood of conversion, with English users converting significantly more often than French or Spanish users.

Step 1: Define null and alternative hypotheses¶

Let's formulate the null and alternative hypothesis for the above problem

Let p1,p2,p3 be the proportions of converted users for English, French and Spanish respectively.

We will test the null hypothesis

$H_0: p1 = p2 = p3$

against the alternative hypothesis

$H_a: $ At least one language group's conversion proportion is different from the rest.

Step 2: Select Appropriate test¶

To test the dependence between two categorical variables, a Chi-square test for independence is used.

Let's test whether the assumptions are satisfied or not¶

  • Categorical variables - Yes
  • Expected value of the number of sample observations in each level of the variable is at least 5 - Yes, the number of observations in each level is greater than 5.
  • Random sampling from the population - Yes, we are informed that the collected sample is a random sample.

Step 3: Decide the significance level¶

As mentioned in the Business Context the level of significane (alpha) has been decided as 0.05

Step 4: Collect and prepare data¶

Let's prepare the data for the test. To perform the chi-squared test of independance, we need to create the contingency table, for which we will use pd.crosstab()

In [133]:
# prepare a contingency table to perform the test
contingency_table_2
Out[133]:
converted no yes
language_preferred
English 11 21
French 19 15
Spanish 16 18

Step 5: Calculate the p-value¶

We will use the chi2_contingency function from the scipy stats library to perform the hypothesis test. It takes the contingency table as an input and returns the test statistic, p-value, degrees of freedom, and the expected frequencies as the output.

In [134]:
# import the required test function
from scipy.stats import chi2_contingency

# calculate the test results
chi, p_value, dof, expected = chi2_contingency(contingency_table_2)

print("Test Statistic =",chi)
print("p-value =",p_value)
print("Degrees of freedom =",dof)
print("Expected frequencies \n", expected)

Test Statistic = 3.0930306905370832
p-value = 0.2129888748754345
Degrees of freedom = 2
Expected frequencies 
 [[14.72 17.28]
 [15.64 18.36]
 [15.64 18.36]]

Step 6: Compare the p-value with $\alpha$¶

In [135]:
# print the conclusion based on p-value
if p_value < 0.05:
    print(f'As the p-value {p_value} is less than the level of significance, we reject the null hypothesis.')
else:
    print(f'As the p-value {p_value} is greater than the level of significance, we fail to reject the null hypothesis.')

As the p-value 0.2129888748754345 is greater than the level of significance, we fail to reject the null hypothesis.

Step 7: Draw inference¶

Since the p-value is less than the 5% significance level, we fail to reject the null hypothesis. Hence, based on the statistical evidence we cannot say that the language preferences and conversion rates are dependent on each other.

Question 4: Is the mean time spent on the new page same for the different language users?¶

In [136]:
#fetching the time_spent_on_the_page grouped by language_preferred for the treatment group only
data[data['group']=='treatment'].groupby(['language_preferred'])['time_spent_on_the_page'].mean()

/tmp/ipykernel_913/2604745406.py:2: FutureWarning: The default of observed=False is deprecated and will be changed to True in a future version of pandas. Pass observed=False to retain current behavior or observed=True to adopt the future default and silence this warning.
  data[data['group']=='treatment'].groupby(['language_preferred'])['time_spent_on_the_page'].mean()
Out[136]:
time_spent_on_the_page
language_preferred
English 6.663750
French 6.196471
Spanish 5.835294

Visualizing the data¶

In [137]:

df_t = data[data['group']=='treatment']
print(df_t.shape)
print(df_t.sample(5))

plt.figure(figsize=(5,5))
sns.displot(data=df_t, x='time_spent_on_the_page', kde=True, hue='language_preferred')

  data[data['group']=='treatment'].groupby(['language_preferred'])['time_spent_on_the_page'].describe()
Out[138]:
count mean std min 25% 50% 75% max
language_preferred

French 17.0 6.196471 1.933394 3.30 5.2500 5.420 7.2700 10.71

Let's write the null and alternative hypothesis¶

Let $\mu_1, \mu_2, \mu_3$ be the means of time_spent_on_the_page by users in the treatment group with the preferred language English, French and Spanish respectively.

We will test the null hypothesis

$H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2 = \mu_3$

against the alternative hypothesis

$H_a: $ At least one mean time_spent_on_the_page is different from the rest.

In [139]:
# mean of carbon emission at different levels of the fuel_type factor
print(data[data['group']=='treatment'].groupby(['language_preferred'])['time_spent_on_the_page'].mean())

# draw the boxplot for visualization
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize = (6,6))
a = sns.boxplot(x= "language_preferred", y = 'time_spent_on_the_page' , data = df_t, hue = 'language_preferred')
a.set_title("Time_spent_on_the_page w.r.t. Language_preferred", fontsize=15)
plt.show()

/tmp/ipykernel_913/1633354092.py:2: FutureWarning: The default of observed=False is deprecated and will be changed to True in a future version of pandas. Pass observed=False to retain current behavior or observed=True to adopt the future default and silence this warning.
  print(data[data['group']=='treatment'].groupby(['language_preferred'])['time_spent_on_the_page'].mean())

language_preferred
English    6.663750
French     6.196471
Spanish    5.835294
Name: time_spent_on_the_page, dtype: float64

Now, the normality and equality of variance assumptions need to be checked.

For testing of normality, Shapiro-Wilk’s test is applied to the response variable.

For equality of variance, Levene test is applied to the response variable.

Shapiro-Wilk’s test¶

We will test the null hypothesis

$H_0:$ Time_Spent_on_the_page follows a normal distribution against

against the alternative hypothesis

$H_a:$ Time_Spent_on_the_page does not follow a normal distribution

In [140]:
# Assumption 1: Normality
# import the required function
from scipy import stats

# find the p-value
w, p_value = stats.shapiro(df_t['time_spent_on_the_page'])
print('The p-value is', p_value)

The p-value is 0.8040040364746845

Since p-value of the test is very large, we fail to reject the null hypothesis that the response follows the normal distribution.

Levene’s test¶

We will test the null hypothesis

$H_0$: All the population variances are equal

against the alternative hypothesis

$H_a$: At least one variance is different from the rest

In [141]:
#Assumption 2: Homogeneity of Variance
#import the required function
from scipy.stats import levene
statistic, p_value = levene( df_t['time_spent_on_the_page'][df_t['language_preferred']=="English"],
                                   df_t['time_spent_on_the_page'][df_t['language_preferred']=="French"],
                                   df_t['time_spent_on_the_page'][df_t['language_preferred']=="Spanish"])
# find the p-value
print('The p-value is', p_value)

The p-value is 0.46711357711340173

Since the p-value is large, we fail to reject the null hypothesis of homogeneity of variances.

Let's test whether the assumptions are satisfied or not

  • The populations are normally distributed - Yes, the normality assumption is verified using the Shapiro-Wilk’s test.
  • Samples are independent simple random samples - Yes, we are informed that the collected sample is a simple random sample.
  • Population variances are equal - Yes, the homogeneity of variance assumption is verified using the Levene's test.

Let's find the p-value¶

The f_oneway() function of Scipy will be used to compute the test statistic and p-value.

In [142]:
#import the required function
from scipy.stats import f_oneway

# perform one-way anova test
test_stat, p_value = f_oneway(df_t.loc[df_t['language_preferred'] == 'English', 'time_spent_on_the_page'],
                              df_t.loc[df_t['language_preferred'] == 'French', 'time_spent_on_the_page'],
                              df_t.loc[df_t['language_preferred'] == 'Spanish', 'time_spent_on_the_page'])
print('The p-value is ' + str(p_value))

The p-value is 0.43204138694325955

Comparing the p-value with $\alpha$¶

In [143]:
# print the conclusion based on p-value
if p_value < 0.05:
    print(f'As the p-value {p_value} is less than the level of significance, we reject the null hypothesis.')
else:
    print(f'As the p-value {p_value} is greater than the level of significance, we fail to reject the null hypothesis.')

As the p-value 0.43204138694325955 is greater than the level of significance, we fail to reject the null hypothesis.

Draw inference¶

As the p-value is much higher than the significance level, we fail to reject the null hypothesis. Hence, based on the statistical evidence we cannot conclude that the mean time spent differs across language preferences for the users of the new landing page.

Actionable Insights and Conclusions¶

Actionable Insights¶

  1. New Landing Page vs. Old Landing Page (Engagement and Conversion):

Key Finding: The new landing page (treatment group) significantly outperforms the old landing page (control group) in two critical aspects:

  • Time Spent: Users, on average, spend more time on the new landing page. The statistical test confirmed that the mean time spent on the new page is greater than on the old page.

  • Conversion Rate: The two-proportion z-test confirmed that the conversion rate for the new landing page (66%) is significantly higher than for the old landing page (42%). Users of the new landing page spend more time on the page and convert more frequently.

Actionable Insight: Strongly recommend implementing the new landing page across all user segments. It is demonstrably more engaging and effective in converting users into subscribers. This change should directly contribute to the company's objective of acquiring more subscribers.

  1. Language Preferred and Conversion Status:

Key Finding: The analysis found no significant statistical evidence to suggest that conversion rates depend on the preferred language. While some visual differences were observed (English users showed a higher conversion rate in the sample), these differences were not statistically significant.

Actionable Insight: Based on this data, there is no immediate need to tailor conversion strategies or landing page content specifically for different language groups concerning conversion rates. The current design of the new landing page appears to be effective across the English, French, and Spanish speaking user base.

  1. Language Preferred and Time Spent on Page:

Key Finding: Similar to conversion rates, the mean time spent on the new page does not significantly differ among users with different language preferences (English, French, Spanish).

Actionable Insight: This reinforces the previous point: the engagement level of the new landing page is consistent across the tested language groups. This suggests the design and content are broadly appealing, and no language-specific modifications are required to maintain engagement.

Overall Conclusion:¶

The new landing page is a clear winner, demonstrating improved user engagement and conversion rates. Its effectiveness is consistent across different language preferences, simplifying deployment. E-news Express should proceed with a full rollout of the new landing page to maximize subscriber acquisition.
